hero.title: Anu Thapa
hero.subtitle: Transforming complex information into clear, user-friendly documentation.
hero.headline: Clarity Through Technical Writing

about.headline: Writing that bridges the gap between complexity and understanding.
about.bio: I am a Brisbane-based Technical Writer with a passion for turning dense, technical subject matter into documentation that developers, end users, and stakeholders can actually use. With roots in Butwal, Nepal, I bring a global perspective and a meticulous, research-driven approach to every project. Over the past several years I have documented APIs, SDKs, and enterprise software for product teams across fintech, health-tech, and developer-tooling companies.
about.philosophy: Documentation is a product, not an afterthought. I believe great technical writing respects the reader's time, anticipates their questions, and removes friction from the learning curve. My process blends structured authoring, information architecture, and user empathy to produce documentation that scales with the product.

portfolio.title: Payments API Reference
portfolio.description: A complete REST API reference for a payments platform, with authentication, webhooks, and sandbox testing.
portfolio.content:
## What this reference covers

A complete REST API reference for a payments platform, documenting every endpoint with authentication, idempotency, pagination, and signed webhooks.

## How it is structured

- Every endpoint includes a purpose, required scopes, and request and response schemas
- Examples are provided in cURL, Node, and Python so integrators can copy and adapt
- Error codes are documented with remediation guidance, not just a status number

## Why it works

Developers consistently complete their first successful call within minutes because the docs treat the happy path and the failure path with equal care.

portfolio.title: Mobile SDK Quickstart
portfolio.description: A multi-language quickstart for a mobile analytics SDK with integration tutorials.
portfolio.content:
## Goal

Get a developer from install to first tracked event in under ten minutes.

## What is inside

- Platform-specific setup for Swift and Kotlin
- A minimal, runnable sample for each core method
- Clear guidance on initialization, identity, and lifecycle

## Outcome

SDK guides reduce integration time and cut friction-related support tickets by meeting developers where they are.

portfolio.title: Clinical Platform User Manual
portfolio.description: An end-to-end user manual for a hospital scheduling system, localized for APAC markets.
portfolio.content:
## Audience

Clinical schedulers, roster coordinators, and hospital administrators across APAC.

## Approach

- Task-based topics mapped to real workflows
- Plain-language steps validated with end users
- Localization-ready structure for regional variants

## Result

A manual that staff can trust during a busy shift, not just during training.

portfolio.title: Platform v3 Release Notes
portfolio.description: Structured release notes communicating breaking changes, migrations, and deprecations.
portfolio.content:
## What changed

This release separates breaking changes from enhancements so teams can plan upgrades confidently.

## Included

- Breaking changes with before and after migration steps
- New capabilities and deprecations with timelines
- A consolidated upgrade checklist

## Why it matters

Clear release notes turn a risky upgrade into a managed project.

portfolio.title: Support Knowledge Base
portfolio.description: A searchable knowledge base that cut support tickets by 30% for a SaaS product.
portfolio.content:
## Purpose

A searchable knowledge base that deflects repetitive support questions.

## How it is built

- A consistent article template and metadata taxonomy
- Troubleshooting articles written from real tickets
- Links to deeper docs where needed

## Impact

Support tickets dropped by 30 percent after launch.

portfolio.content:

blog.title: Why Docs-as-Code Is the Future of Technical Writing
blog.excerpt: Treating documentation like softwareâ€”versioned, reviewed, and shipped in CIâ€”unlocks quality and speed.
blog.content:
Docs-as-Code is the practice of writing, reviewing, and shipping documentation with the same tooling you use for software.

## Why it matters

Treating docs as code closes the gap between what the product does and what you say it does. When documentation lives next to the code, it is versioned, reviewable, and testable.

- Fewer stale pages, because docs fail CI when examples break
- Faster reviews, because writers and engineers collaborate in the same workflow
- Reusable content, authored in Markdown or AsciiDoc

## How to start without overwhelming your team

You do not need a perfect pipeline on day one. Begin with three small moves:

- Move docs into the repository alongside code
- Adopt a linter such as Vale or markdownlint in CI
- Publish with a static site generator like Docusaurus or VitePress

## Common pitfalls

The biggest failure mode is tooling first, culture second. Win hearts with a single high-value doc set before automating everything.

blog.title: 2024 Trends in API Documentation
blog.excerpt: From AI-assisted authoring to OpenAPI-driven portals, here is what is shaping API docs this year.
blog.content:
API documentation in 2024 is no longer a static reference pasted at the bottom of a README. It is an interactive product that developers judge before they ever call your endpoint.

## Interactive, not static

OpenAPI-driven portals now generate try-it-out consoles, client SDKs, and changelogs automatically. If your API ships without a live explorer, you are losing evaluators at the door.

## AI-assisted authoring

Large language models are changing the first draft. Writers use them to expand terse parameter tables into plain-language explanations, then apply judgment and accuracy reviews.

- Use AI to draft, never to publish unverified
- Keep a human in the loop for security and correctness
- Treat generated examples as code: test them

## What stays constant

No matter the tooling, the fundamentals hold: accurate request and response samples, honest error documentation, and authentication that a beginner can actually follow.

blog.title: A Practical Review of Modern Authoring Tools
blog.excerpt: Markdown, Docusaurus, MadCap Flare, or Confluence? A field guide to picking the right tool.
blog.content:
Choosing documentation tooling is a decision about your audience and your team, not about which product won a popularity contest. Here is a practical field guide.

## Markdown plus a static site generator

Best for: developer-focused docs, open-source projects, and teams comfortable in Git.

- Pros: fast, portable, cheap, version-controlled
- Cons: limited enterprise features, you build the CMS yourself

## Docusaurus

Best for: product docs that need versioning and i18n out of the box.

- Pros: React-based, great search, MDX support
- Cons: heavier than plain Markdown, build step required

## MadCap Flare

Best for: large regulated manuals and single-source publishing to PDF and HTML.

- Pros: powerful reuse, conditional content, PDF output
- Cons: commercial license, steeper learning curve

## Confluence

Best for: internal engineering and ops documentation.

- Pros: collaborative, permissioned, integrates with Jira
- Cons: becomes a maze without strong information architecture

## The honest takeaway

Pick the simplest tool your audience can consume and your team will actually maintain. Consistency beats sophistication.
